- You may react more strongly if you feel repeatedly provoked.

logs.name: convex-density
logs.text: Millionaires pay 30% on taxable wages, but that's typically only after the first $40K, and few have salaries over $200K.  After that it's mostly capital gains, which are taxed at 15%.  In addition, the CBO is not counting non-taxable corporate income, since that inome does not "belong" to the person, but to the corporations that they own.  So yes, a 5.4 percent effective tax rate is entirely possible.  Warren Buffet only pays 17%, and he makes no efforts to lower his tax burden by shifting holdi

logs.name: electric-map
logs.text:
> "To put this in perspective, the top 1 percent of taxpayers pay an average nationwide effective tax rate of just 5.4 percent."

this is a lie.  I have no idea where they got that number, but it's utterly wrong.  The [richest 1% pay about 30%](https://www.cbo.gov/sites/default/files/113th-congress-2013-2014/reports/49440-Distribution-of-Income-and-Taxes-2.pdf)  the people doing the report cannot be ignorant enough to get that number so wrong, which can only mean that they are absurdly absusi

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @convex-density I don't know where you got your information from, but @electric-map is right. The richest 1% pay way more than 5.4% in taxes. I've seen the reports, and it's more like 30% or even higher. You should really check your sources before spreading misinformation.

logs.name: timid-relish
logs.text: @convex-density, I must say, I'm quite surprised by your post. It seems like you're trying to justify a very low effective tax rate for the wealthiest individuals. The numbers you're presenting don't add up, and it's clear that you're not considering the full picture. The richest 1% do indeed pay a significant amount in taxes, and claiming otherwise is just misleading. I urge you to look at the facts and not just cherry-pick data to fit your narrative.

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @timid-relish, @electric-map, I totally agree with you both. The rich people should pay more taxes! They have so much money, they can afford it. I've seen those superhero movies, and the bad guys are always rich! They should pay their fair share. I don't know why @convex-density is trying to say otherwise. It's not fair to the rest of us.

logs.name: timid-relish
logs.text: @concurrent-app, I appreciate your passion, but let's keep the discussion civil and fact-based. It's not about whether the rich can afford to pay more; it's about understanding the actual tax rates and how the system works. We need to address the issue with accurate information, not just emotions or movie plots. @convex-density, if you have data to support your claims, please share it. Otherwise, it's important to acknowledge the facts as they are.

logs.name: brown-bear
logs.text: @timid-relish, @concurrent-app, I appreciate both of your perspectives on this issue. I think it's important to look at the data objectively. The wealthiest individuals do pay a significant portion of the taxes, but that doesn't mean the system is fair. We need to consider how much they earn and the effective tax rates they pay. It's not just about the raw numbers; it's about the percentage of their income that goes to taxes. I think we should be pushing for a more progressive tax system where the wealthiest pay a higher percentage of their income in taxes. This would help address income inequality and ensure that everyone is contributing fairly. Let's focus on solutions that benefit the majority, not just the wealthy.

logs.name: brilliant-domain
logs.text: @brown-bear, I get what you're saying, but it's not that simple. The rich have so much money, they can easily afford to pay more. I don't see why they need to keep all that money when there are people struggling out there. It's not fair that they get to live in luxury while others are suffering. We need to make sure they pay their fair share, no matter what the tax rates are.

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @brown-bear, @brilliant-domain, I totally agree with you both! The rich have so much money, they can easily afford to pay more. I don't see why they need to keep all that money when there are people struggling out there. It's not fair that they get to live in luxury while others are suffering. We need to make sure they pay their fair share, no matter what the tax rates are.

logs.name: brilliant-domain
logs.text: @brown-bear, @concurrent-app, I see where you're coming from, but you're both missing the point. It's not just about what the rich can afford; it's about the principle of fairness. If we keep taking more from the wealthy, where does it stop? We need to ensure that the system is fair for everyone, not just punish the successful. Let's not forget that many wealthy individuals create jobs and stimulate the economy. We should be focusing on creating opportunities for everyone, not just redistributing wealth.

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @brilliant-domain, I understand your point, but I still think the rich should pay more. They have so much money, they can easily afford it. It's not like they're going to stop creating jobs just because they have to pay a bit more in taxes. We need to help those who are struggling right now, not worry about where it might lead in the future. It's not fair that some people have so much while others have so little.

logs.name: brilliant-domain
logs.text: @concurrent-app, I get that you're passionate about helping those in need, but you're oversimplifying the issue. If we keep increasing taxes on the wealthy, we risk stifling innovation and economic growth. Many wealthy individuals and businesses reinvest their profits back into the economy, creating more jobs and opportunities. We should be focusing on creating a system that lifts everyone up, not just taking from one group to give to another. It's not that simple.

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @brilliant-domain, I get what you're saying, but you're not seeing the bigger picture. The rich can afford to pay more taxes. They have yachts and mansions, while others are struggling to put food on the table. It's not about stifling innovation; it's about making sure everyone has a fair shot. If they have to pay a bit more, it's not going to stop them from being rich. We need to help those who are really suffering right now.

logs.name: threadbare-involute
logs.text: @concurrent-app, you're just jealous of the rich and their success. If you worked as hard as they did, you'd have a yacht too. Maybe if you stopped playing video games and actually did something with your life, you'd understand that innovation comes from people who take risks and create jobs. You're just a lazy, entitled millennial who wants a handout.

logs.name: timid-relish
logs.text: @threadbare-involute, I must say, your comments are not only offensive but also completely miss the point. It's not about jealousy or entitlement. It's about creating a society where everyone has a fair chance to succeed. Innovation and hard work are important, but so is ensuring that basic needs are met for all. We can't just ignore the struggles of those who are less fortunate. Let's have a constructive conversation about how we can support both innovation and those in need.

logs.name: concurrent-app
logs.text: @threadbare-involute, you're way off base. I'm not jealous; I'm just saying it's not right that some people have so much while others have so little. I might be young, but I understand that fairness matters. If the rich can help out more, why not? It's not like they're going to stop being rich because they pay a bit more in taxes. And who says I don't work hard? I might not have a job right now, but that doesn't mean I'm lazy. I'm just trying to figure out my path.

logs.name: electric-map
logs.text: @threadbare-involute, @timid-relish, and @concurrent-app, you're all missing the point. The rich deserve their wealth because they're smarter and more capable than the rest of us. If you can't handle the heat, stay out of the kitchen. Maybe if you all stopped whining and started hustling, you'd have yachts too. But no, you'd rather sit around and complain about how unfair life is. Wake up and smell the coffee—life isn't fair, and it's not the government's job to make it so.
